target.title: Fix Azure Responses model alias routing
target.body:
## Summary

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix for embedded-agent provider routing and model alias owner selection.
- Root cause: The resolver made the `azure-openai-responses` canonicalization decision from alias-level API adapter and model-row presence alone, instead of separating Azure endpoint ownership surface from manifest-suppressed models. That caused codex-only models to be owned by Azure in one path, provider-level Azure custom deployment names to be canonicalized back to OpenAI in another path, and manifest-only Azure alias rows to keep Azure ownership even when no Azure endpoint was configured.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The fix moves the preserve-versus-canonicalize decision to the resolver invariant that owns the behavior: keep a transport alias when the requested model has actual Azure endpoint surface through config or alias metadata, but still canonicalize manifest-suppressed alias models and endpoint-less aliases back to the target owner. This preserves Azure routes for real Azure Responses models and custom deployment names while restoring OpenAI-owned fallback and codex-only OAuth guidance.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High. The current patch directly covers the CI-failing codex-only owner path, the configured-Azure/no-matching-model-row edge, provider-level Azure Responses config without `models[]`, custom Azure deployment names, endpoint-less Azure manifest aliases, and the existing configured Azure `gpt-5.5` route that must stay on Azure.
- Patch quality notes: The alias retention logic now distinguishes configured model rows, manifest alias rows, configured endpoint surface, alias endpoint surface, and manifest suppressions instead of treating all transport aliases the same; the configured provider override also preserves the requested provider owner when it applies Azure transport over a discovered OpenAI catalog model. Related open PR / competition scan: this is an update to existing author-owned PR #93833 for linked issue #93781, not a new competing canonical PR, and the public contract surface remains limited to existing model config fields and resolver ownership.

What problem does this PR solve?

`azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` embedded-agent/probe routes were being treated like a pure OpenAI provider alias, so the request could resolve through OpenAI provider/auth metadata instead of the configured Azure Responses provider. The first fix preserved every transport-overriding alias on the requested provider, but CI showed that was too broad: `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` then reported the codex-only OAuth error as an Azure Responses model instead of the OpenAI-owned model.

Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is an auth/provider-selection bug: Azure Responses routes need to keep Azure provider identity for real Azure deployments, while OpenAI codex-only models still need OpenAI-owned OAuth guidance. Mixing those owners gives users the wrong remediation path and can mask the actual credential boundary.

What is the intended outcome?

The resolver now keeps `azure-openai-responses` when the requested model has actual Azure Responses endpoint surface: a configured Azure base URL, a matching configured model row with endpoint surface, or alias metadata that declares an endpoint. If the alias/model is explicitly manifest-suppressed, such as the codex-only model, or the manifest alias only names an API adapter without an Azure endpoint, the alias can canonicalize back to its OpenAI owner so the error or fallback does not mix Azure ownership with the OpenAI API endpoint.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change the OpenAI plugin manifest, provider runtime implementations, auth profile storage, public config shape, migrations, schema, or Azure API behavior. It only tightens embedded-agent model alias owner selection.

What does success look like?

- `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` with Azure Responses config still resolves as provider `azure-openai-responses`, api `azure-openai-responses`, and the configured Azure base URL.
- `azure-openai-responses/customer-gpt-deployment` with provider-level Azure transport config resolves under the Azure provider owner instead of `openai/customer-gpt-deployment`.
- `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` with no Azure model row reports the OpenAI codex-only owner: `openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark`, including when provider-level Azure transport config exists.
- `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` without configured or declared Azure endpoint surface canonicalizes back to the OpenAI owner instead of producing an Azure-owned model pointed at the OpenAI API endpoint.
- Pure provider aliases such as `moonshot-ai -> moonshot` continue to canonicalize normally.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please focus on whether the owner boundary is correct: transport aliases are preserved only for models that actually have alias/config catalog surface, while missing alias models fall back to the target provider owner for errors and guidance.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #93781

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related CI failure: `checks-node-agentic-agents-embedded` on the previous PR head failed because `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` produced an Azure-owned error string instead of the expected OpenAI codex-only OAuth hint.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request on the PR. The linked issue and failing CI identify the provider/auth routing regression.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` model/probe routing keeps the Azure Responses provider, API, base URL, and auth route instead of falling back to OpenAI; this update also restores the OpenAI owner for missing codex-only Azure alias requests.
- Real environment tested: Prior real OpenClaw `models status --probe` proof used a local mock Responses server on Darwin 25.5.0 arm64, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw v2026.6.8. Current CI repair validation ran on Linux with Node/pnpm in the PR worktree.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  # Prior real routing/probe proof with a temporary OpenClaw config containing:
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.api=azure-openai-responses
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.baseUrl=<mock Responses server>/openai/v1
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.models[0].id=gpt-5.5
  OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp-home> \
    OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp-openclaw.json> \
    OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp-state> \
    pnpm openclaw models status --json --probe \
      --probe-provider azure-openai-responses \
      --probe-timeout 10000 \
      --probe-max-tokens 1

  pnpm exec vitest run \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts

  pnpm check:test-types
  ```
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  ```json
  {
    "command": "pnpm openclaw models status --json --probe --probe-provider azure-openai-responses --probe-timeout 10000 --probe-max-tokens 1",
    "exitCode": 0,
    "resolvedDefault": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
    "defaultModel": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
    "missingProvidersInUse": [],
    "probe": {
      "totalTargets": 1,
      "results": [
        {
          "provider": "azure-openai-responses",
          "model": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
          "source": "models.json",
          "status": "ok"
        }
      ]
    },
    "mockRequests": [
      {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "/openai/v1/responses?api-version=preview",
        "authorizationPresent": false,
        "apiKeyHeaderPresent": true,
        "bodyModel": "gpt-5.5",
        "stream": true,
        "maxOutputTokens": 1
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
  ```text
  pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts -t "canonicalizes Azure manifest aliases without configured endpoints back to OpenAI"
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  2 passed | 30 skipped (32)

  pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts -t "provider-level azure deployment names|provider-level azure codex-only"
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  4 passed | 56 skipped (60)

  pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts -t "keeps transport-overriding manifest aliases|keeps provider-level Azure transport aliases"
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests  4 passed | 236 skipped (240)

  pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts
  Test Files  6 passed (6)
  Tests  332 passed (332)

  pnpm check:test-types
  exitCode=0
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The real OpenClaw probe path created one Azure Responses probe target, reported `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5`, and sent the request to the configured Azure Responses base URL with an API-key header. The current regression validation also shows provider-level Azure custom deployment names stay on `azure-openai-responses`, provider-level codex-only requests still report `openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark`, and configured Azure Responses routes stay on Azure for both explicit model rows and provider-level Azure transport config without `models[]`.
- What was not tested: Live Azure account credentials.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The routing/probe proof uses a local mock Responses server to validate OpenClaw provider routing without external credentials. The current Linux environment could validate the CI-failing resolver behavior and typecheck, but did not have live Azure credentials for a real Azure account probe.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The previous PR head failed `checks-node-agentic-agents-embedded` because the codex-only test expected `Unknown model: openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark...` but received `Unknown model: azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex-spark...`.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.forward-compat.errors-and-overrides.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts \
  src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts

pnpm check:test-types
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added coverage for `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.3-codex-spark` when the Azure provider is configured but does not declare that model row; the error must come from `openai/gpt-5.3-codex-spark`.
- Added coverage for provider-level Azure Responses config without `models[]`; a discovered OpenAI catalog model still resolves under the requested `azure-openai-responses` owner when Azure transport overrides are configured.
- Added coverage for provider-level Azure custom deployment names, which must keep the Azure provider owner even when the deployment name is not in the bundled OpenAI catalog.
- Added coverage that provider-level Azure codex-only aliases remain OpenAI-owned through manifest suppression instead of being treated like custom Azure deployments.
- Added coverage that endpoint-less manifest Azure aliases canonicalize back to OpenAI instead of keeping Azure ownership while inheriting the OpenAI API endpoint.
- Kept the existing Azure Responses route coverage for `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5`, which must stay on the Azure provider, Azure API adapter, and configured Azure base URL.
- Existing pure provider alias coverage still protects non-transport aliases such as `moonshot-ai -> moonshot`.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The CI shard `checks-node-agentic-agents-embedded` failed on the prior PR head because the broad transport-override preservation kept the codex-only OpenAI model under `azure-openai-responses` for the unknown-model error owner.

If no test was added, why not?

A test was added for the configured-Azure/no-matching-model-row case, and the existing CI-failing codex-only test now passes.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Error ownership for missing Azure alias models now falls back to the target provider when the Azure alias has no matching model surface, while real Azure alias model routes keep Azure provider identity.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No. Existing config fields are interpreted more precisely; no new config, environment variable, schema, migration, or default value is introduced.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes, in the narrow provider/auth routing sense: model owner selection now keeps Azure auth routing only for actual Azure alias models and keeps OpenAI OAuth guidance for OpenAI-owned codex-only models.

What is the highest-risk area?

Provider owner selection for transport-overriding manifest aliases.

How is that risk mitigated?

The fix uses configured Azure endpoint surface as the source of truth for Azure ownership, model rows as model-specific routing evidence, and manifest suppression as the explicit blocklist for models that must remain target-provider owned. Related tests cover the boundary: configured Azure `gpt-5.5` and custom deployments stay Azure, endpoint-less Azure manifest aliases canonicalize back to OpenAI, and codex-only `gpt-5.3-codex-spark` reports through OpenAI even with provider-level Azure transport config.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after CI re-runs on the updated head.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI needs to re-run on the updated head.
- Live Azure account testing was not performed; the supplied real behavior proof uses a local mock Responses server to validate OpenClaw routing/probe behavior without external credentials.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed the visible CI failure from `checks-node-agentic-agents-embedded` by restoring the OpenAI-owned codex-only error path.
- RF-001: The prior remote ClawSweeper review did not complete, so it is treated as a bot advisory pending a fresh review on the updated head.
- Addressed the local ClawSweeper findings that provider-level Azure Responses config without `models[]` could either keep Azure transport fields while returning an OpenAI provider owner, canonicalize Azure custom deployment names to `openai/<deployment>` before configured fallback, or keep an endpoint-less Azure manifest alias on the Azure owner while inheriting the OpenAI API endpoint. The resolver now preserves the requested provider owner for configured Azure endpoint surface and custom deployment names, while manifest-suppressed codex-only models and endpoint-less aliases still fall back to the OpenAI owner.

target.title: fix: #94224 允许 cron lane 静默回复，避免空回复 FailoverError
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224 — Cron jobs that intentionally produce empty assistant replies fail with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response. (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")`.

The auto-reply path (DM/group chat) supports silent replies via `silentReplyPolicy`. A cron job runs in an isolated lane with no chat context — there is no user to "reply" to. An empty assistant response from a condition-check-only cron job is expected behavior (e.g., "check a condition, only notify if it changed"). Yet the cron executor never passed `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent`, so every silent tick threw `FailoverError` and appeared as `status: error` in `cron list`.

## Change

`src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts` — +2 lines, same flag in both runner paths.

| Location | Line | Change |
|----------|------|--------|
| CLI runner params | 321 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: !params.deliveryRequested` |
| Embedded agent runner params | 378 | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: !params.deliveryRequested` |

**Gating on `deliveryRequested`:**

The flag is NOT unconditional. It is gated by whether the cron job requested delivery:

| Scenario | `deliveryRequested` | `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` | Behavior |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|----------|
| `--no-deliver` condition-check cron | `false`/`undefined` | `true` | Empty reply → `status: ok` (silent success) |
| `--deliver` announce/webhook cron | `true` | `false` | Empty reply → `FailoverError` (unchanged) |

This preserves the existing contract: cron jobs that are expected to produce output for delivery still fail on empty replies. Only cron jobs with no delivery target — where silence means "nothing to report" — get the silent-success behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed:** Cron jobs with empty assistant replies throw `FailoverError` and show `status: error` in `cron list`. The same cron job, when configured with `--no-deliver`, should complete with `status: ok` — an empty reply means "nothing to report", not a failure.

**Real environment tested:**
Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64, Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw fix branch @ 34b39fbf, DeepSeek V4 (`deepseek-chat`) as live LLM backend, Gateway on port 18790.

**Before-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```bash
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 3f8a1b2c-...
Run: 2026-06-17T16:50:12.334+08:00
Status: error
Error: FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
       (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27102 → 1
Duration: 3203ms
```

The model output 1 token (whitespace) → `trim()` → empty string → `FailoverError`. The cron job was designed to only alert on a condition match and stay silent otherwise. Every silent tick produced an error.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd ~/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-94224-cron-empty-reply

# Start Gateway
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=~/.openclaw/openclaw.json \
  node openclaw.mjs gateway --port 18790 &

# Create silent-alert cron: only whitespace output → empty after trim()
node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --no-deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# Wait for 2 cron ticks, then check runs
node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <job-id>
```

**After-fix evidence (real terminal output):**

```
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id 753509ee-...
Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.141+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27088 → 1
Duration: 3123ms

Run: 2026-06-18T09:24:03.114+08:00
Status: ok
Model: deepseek/deepseek-chat
Tokens: 27086 → 1
Duration: 3097ms
```

Both ticks complete with `Status: ok`. The model produced 1 output token (whitespace only → `trim()` → empty string). With `deliveryRequested` falsy (`--no-deliver`), the cron executor sets `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` and skips the `FailoverError` throw.

**Deliver-mode cron still errors (gated behavior):**

```bash
$ node openclaw.mjs cron add \
  --url ws://127.0.0.1:18790 --token "<token>" \
  --cron "*/3 * * * *" --session isolated --deliver \
  --message "Output only a single whitespace character. Nothing else."

# After cron fires:
$ node openclaw.mjs cron runs --id <job-id>
Run: 2026-06-18T09:30:15.421+08:00
Status: error
Error: FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response.
       (reason: empty_response, lane: "cron")
```

With `--deliver`, `deliveryRequested` is truthy → `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: false` → `FailoverError` is preserved. The gating works correctly.

**Existing test preserved:**

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  13 passed (13)
```

Including `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` — the existing contract that cron empty replies should error is unchanged for the `runCliAgent` API surface.

**Observed result after the fix:** `--no-deliver` cron jobs with empty/near-empty LLM output complete with `status: ok`. `--deliver` cron jobs (announce/webhook) still throw `FailoverError` on empty replies. The gating aligns with the semantic: if no one is waiting for output, silence is success.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end with Anthropic provider (requires separate API key). The DeepSeek proof validates the full pipeline: Gateway cron scheduler → isolated agent run → LLM call → empty reply → flag check → `status: ok`. The flag is provider-agnostic — it gates a post-LLM string check.

## Tests and validation

| Check | Result |
|-------|--------|
| `cli-runner.before-agent-reply-cron.test.ts` | ✅ 13 passed (existing contract preserved) |
| `openclaw health --json` | ✅ `ok: true` |
| Type check (`tsgo`) | ✅ clean |
| Lint (`oxlint`) | ✅ clean |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No` — cron execution status for empty replies changes from `error` to `ok` only for `--no-deliver` cron jobs where the empty reply is intentional. `--deliver` cron jobs are unaffected.)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No` — no new config keys, no migration, no env vars. Uses existing `deliveryRequested` field.)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Cron jobs configured with both `--no-deliver` and an expectation (via external monitoring) that `status: error` signals a problem. If someone monitors `cron runs` for errors and their `--no-deliver` cron goes truly silent due to a model issue, they would lose the error signal. In practice, `--no-deliver` means no delivery target is waiting for output, so there is no consumer to notice the difference.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The flag is gated on `deliveryRequested`, not unconditional. `--deliver` cron jobs (the ones with consumers) still error on empty replies.
- All other failure modes (auth errors, model errors, timeout, crash) are unchanged and still surface as `status: error`.
- The existing test `treats empty CLI subprocess output as a failover failure, not a green cron run` continues to pass — the CLI runner API contract is unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review — the gated approach addresses the ClawSweeper [P1] finding by narrowing the flag to `!deliveryRequested` instead of unconditional `true`.

target.title: fix: channels with only {enabled: true} config are skipped at startup
target.body:
## Problem

`hasMeaningfulChannelConfig` in `src/channels/config-presence.ts` filters out channel configs that only contain `{"enabled": true}`. This causes channels to be excluded from `listPotentialConfiguredChannelIds`, and thus not loaded by the Gateway at startup.

`qqbot` channel works because it also has `appId` and `clientSecret` keys. But any channel that only sets `enabled: true` is silently skipped.

## Fix

Added early return `if (value.enabled === true) return true` — if a channel is explicitly enabled, it should always be considered meaningfully configured.

## Before/After

| Config | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `{"enabled": true}` | ❌ skipped | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": true, "appId": "x"}` | ✅ loaded | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": false}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |
| `{}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |

target.title: fix(chat): MEDIA: binary file delivery — proper download for xlsx/csv/zip (Sandpaw Bug #9b)
target.body:
# Sandpaw Bug #9b — file delivery for non-image binary files

Bug #9 covered MIME inference (#77875) and the gateway-managed outgoing
document pipeline (#77877). Bug #9b plugs three remaining gaps in the
plain `MEDIA:` → `/__openclaw__/assistant-media` path that produced Todd
Bolyard's "Save as Webpage Complete" fallback for non-image binary files
on `go-yard.sandpaw.ai`.

## Root cause (one-liner)

`/__openclaw__/assistant-media` streamed xlsx/csv/pdf/zip with the right
`Content-Type` but no `Content-Disposition`, the chat anchor lacked a
`download` attribute, and the new managed `/api/chat/media/outgoing-doc/`
URL was being double-wrapped through assistant-media — together that lets
Safari render the response in-tab and offer Save As "Webpage Complete".

## What goes wrong without this PR

1. `/__openclaw__/assistant-media` sets the right `Content-Type` for
   `xlsx`/`csv`/`pdf`/`zip` but **no `Content-Disposition`**. Safari (and
   other browsers under various preference combinations) renders the
   response in-tab; File→Save offers "Webpage Complete" as the only
   option.
2. The document anchor in `grouped-render.ts` uses `target="_blank"` with
   **no `download` attribute**, so even browsers that respect
   `Content-Disposition` are first asked to open the file in a new tab —
   the gateway hint is the only thing keeping it from rendering.
3. The managed `/api/chat/media/outgoing-doc/` URL produced by the
   gateway (#77877) is **misclassified as a "local" source** and
   double-wrapped through `/__openclaw__/assistant-media`, discarding the
   managed `Content-Disposition: attachment` header.

## What lands

### Server side (`src/gateway/control-ui.ts`)

- `handleControlUiAssistantMediaRequest` sets
  `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="<basename>"` for any
  response whose resolved MIME is not image/audio/video. Images, audio,
  and video keep the default inline disposition because the chat surface
  inlines them. `application/octet-stream` fallbacks also get the
  attachment hint so unclassified content still downloads cleanly.
- `safeAssistantMediaDownloadFilename` mirrors the managed-document
  sibling: strips CR/LF/quote/backslash/slash so the filename is safe
  to interpolate into the header. Unicode is preserved.

### Client side (`ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`)

- Document-kind anchors now render with `download=<label>` alongside the
  existing `target=_blank` / `rel=noreferrer` pair, so clicks trigger
  same-origin save-to-disk with the suggested filename. `target=_blank`
  stays as a graceful fallback for browsers that ignore the `download`
  attribute on cross-origin or sandboxed responses.
- `isLocalAssistantAttachmentSource` also recognizes
  `/api/chat/media/outgoing-doc/` as a non-local URL so the managed-doc
  response from #77877 isn't double-wrapped through assistant-media and
  keeps its `Content-Disposition: attachment` header.

## Tests

### `src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts` (Bug #9b describe block, 6 tests)

- `.xlsx`, `.csv`, `.pdf`, `.zip` each respond 200 with the canonical
  `attachment; filename="<original>"` header.
- `.png` keeps the default inline disposition (no header set).
- Filename sanitization replaces embedded double quotes with `_`.

### `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` (3 new + 1 strengthened)

- Existing "verified local assistant attachments" test now asserts
  `download=test-doc.pdf` on the rendered anchor.
- New "binary document attachments (xlsx/csv/zip)" test renders three
  `MEDIA:` refs and asserts `download=pricing.xlsx`, `export.csv`,
  `bundle.zip` plus the standard mediaTicket-bearing href.
- "preserves same-origin assistant attachments" test now asserts
  `download=test-doc.pdf` on the same-origin `/__openclaw__/media`
  anchor.
- New "managed outgoing-doc URLs" test asserts the
  `/api/chat/media/outgoing-doc/` URL renders without re-wrapping
  through `/__openclaw__/assistant-media` and carries
  `download=pricing.xlsx`.

## Test results

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts` — 156
  passed (52 × 3 environments)
- `pnpm --dir ui exec vitest run src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts` —
  37 passed
- All 11 unit-environment chat UI test files (121 tests) pass
- `oxlint` clean on the four touched files
- `pnpm lint:ui:no-raw-window-open` clean

## Related

- Pairs with #77875 (UI MIME mappings, Bug #9)
- Pairs with #77877 (gateway managed document pipeline, Bug #9)
- Sandpaw repo PR `gregstiehl/sandpaw-ai#244` covers the spreadsheet
  skill side (Bug #9a)
- Reported by Todd Bolyard on `go-yard.sandpaw.ai`, 2026-05-05



## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: `/__openclaw__/assistant-media` previously
served `.xlsx`/`.csv`/`.pdf`/`.zip` with the right `Content-Type` but no
`Content-Disposition` header, so Safari rendered them in-tab and offered
"Webpage Complete" as the only Save As format. After this PR every
non-image MIME gets `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="..."`.

**Real environment tested**: macOS 25.3.0 (arm64), Node v22.22.0, branch
`fix/chat-binary-file-delivery-bug-9b` at commit `70700cf707`. Real
`~/.openclaw` state dir, real on-disk fixture files, no vitest, no mocks.
The handler is loaded directly from `src/gateway/control-ui.ts` via an
esbuild bundle and served through `http.createServer()` listening on
`127.0.0.1:8765`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```sh
1. Build a CJS-compatible ESM bundle of the patched module:
node_modules/.bin/esbuild src/gateway/control-ui.ts \
  --bundle --platform=node --format=esm \
  --outfile=_bug9_control_ui.mjs \
  --external:sharp --external:fsevents \
  --banner:js='import { createRequire as __ccr } from "node:module"; const require = __ccr(import.meta.url);'

2. Tiny harness:
cat > _bug9_harness.mjs <<'JS'
import http from "node:http";
import { handleControlUiAssistantMediaRequest } from "./_bug9_control_ui.mjs";
http.createServer(async (req, res) => {
  const handled = await handleControlUiAssistantMediaRequest(req, res, {});
  if (!handled) { res.writeHead(404).end("not handled"); }
}).listen(8765, "127.0.0.1", () => console.log("listening"));
JS
node ./_bug9_harness.mjs &

3. Real fixture files in ~/.openclaw/workspace/bug9-proof/:
--    pricing.xlsx (PK\x03\x04), data.csv, report.pdf, bundle.zip, photo.png

4. Hit each one:
for f in pricing.xlsx data.csv report.pdf bundle.zip photo.png; do
  curl -sIv "http://127.0.0.1:8765/__openclaw__/assistant-media?source=$HOME/.openclaw/workspace/bug9-proof/$f"
done
```

**After-fix evidence**:

`.xlsx` — gets `Content-Disposition: attachment` (the fix):

```
> HEAD /__openclaw__/assistant-media?source=/Users/ada/.openclaw/workspace/bug9-proof/pricing.xlsx HTTP/1.1
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Content-Type: application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.spreadsheetml.sheet
< Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="pricing.xlsx"
< Cache-Control: no-cache
< Content-Length: 2058
```

`.csv`:

```
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Content-Type: text/csv
< Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="data.csv"
< Content-Length: 36
```

`.pdf`:

```
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Content-Type: application/pdf
< Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="report.pdf"
< Content-Length: 53
```

`.zip`:

```
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Content-Type: application/zip
< Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="bundle.zip"
< Content-Length: 1028
```

`.png` — does NOT get `Content-Disposition` (proves the fix is targeted):

```
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Content-Type: image/png
< Cache-Control: no-cache
< Content-Length: 69
```

**Observed result after fix**:

| extension | `Content-Type`                                                     | `Content-Disposition`                  |
|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| `.xlsx`   | `application/vnd.openxmlformats-...spreadsheetml.sheet`            | `attachment; filename="pricing.xlsx"`  |
| `.csv`    | `text/csv`                                                         | `attachment; filename="data.csv"`      |
| `.pdf`    | `application/pdf`                                                  | `attachment; filename="report.pdf"`    |
| `.zip`    | `application/zip`                                                  | `attachment; filename="bundle.zip"`    |
| `.png`    | `image/png`                                                        | *(absent — inline)*                    |

Every binary document now triggers a download with the original filename;
images stay inline as before. This is the wire-level behaviour Todd
Bolyard's browser will see on `go-yard.sandpaw.ai`.

**What was not tested**: I did not exercise the full chat anchor click in
a real browser on `go-yard.sandpaw.ai` itself — I only verified the
gateway response shape end-to-end. The grouped-render `download=` anchor
attribute change is covered by the strengthened `grouped-render.test.ts`
assertions (which run against jsdom).

target.title: fix: Codex startup plugins + WhatsApp history & Docker Codex OAuth
target.body:
## Summary

Two related fixes for **Docker + Codex** operation and **WhatsApp** agents:

1. **Gateway startup loads the plugin that owns the configured primary model** so provider-backed harnesses (e.g. Codex) register **before** channels start handling traffic. This removes `Requested agent harness "codex" is not registered` when the primary model is `codex/…` but the Codex plugin was not part of the previous startup plugin set.

2. **WhatsApp channel** gains a **recent history** path for follow-up actions, plus **Docker / Codex OAuth** wiring (port `1455` for the Codex OAuth callback, provider env passthrough, and a small `openai-codex-auth.sh` helper) so the stack matches documented Codex subscription flows.

## Motivation

- **Codex harness**: Channels could start while the **codex** plugin was still not in the gateway startup list, so the harness never registered in time.
- **WhatsApp**: Agents needed access to **recent chat context** for actions instead of failing blind on follow-ups.
- **Docker**: Codex OAuth (pi-ai callback on `127.0.0.1:1455`) and related envs were not fully reflected in the default compose surface.

## Changes

| Area | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.ts` | Resolve the configured primary model ref, map to **owning plugin IDs** via `resolveOwningPluginIdsForModelRef`, and **merge** those into `resolveGatewayStartupPluginIds` so provider plugins (e.g. codex) load at startup when implied by config. |
| `src/plugins/channel-plugin-ids.test.ts` | Regression test: primary model owned by a provider plugin + auto-enabled plugin vs raw config. |
| `extensions/whatsapp/*` | History fetch / monitor plumbing, types, and channel wiring for **recent inbound history** used by message actions. |
| `docker-compose.yml` | Publish **1455:1455** for Codex OAuth callback; document intent in comments. |
| `scripts/docker/openai-codex-auth.sh` | Small helper for Codex auth flow in Docker. |
| Config / infra | WhatsApp provider schema and message-action spec updates for the new action. |

## How to test

1. **Codex / harness**: Configure primary model `codex/gpt-5.4` (or equivalent) with embedded Codex harness; start gateway. Confirm logs show **codex** in the startup plugin list and **no** harness registration error when Telegram/WhatsApp channels start.
2. **WhatsApp**: Trigger a flow that uses the new **history** action; confirm recent messages are available to the agent path under test.
3. **Docker**: `docker compose … up` with Codex OAuth flow; confirm callback port **1455** is reachable from the host as documented.

## Risk / rollout

- Startup plugin set grows slightly when the primary model implies extra provider plugins; should be limited to **resolved owner IDs** for the configured primary ref.
- WhatsApp changes touch inbound monitor and channel actions—verify in a non-production account first if possible.

## Checklist

- [x] Unit test added for gateway startup plugin resolution (`channel-plugin-ids.test.ts`).
- [ ] Maintainer review for WhatsApp extension surface area.

target.title: [codex] Fix explicit custom web_search provider routing
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes `web_search` routing when `tools.web.search.provider` explicitly names a custom or installed web-search provider.

The agent tool wrapper was resolving late-bound runtime metadata and then allowing that runtime-selected provider to determine whether provider discovery should use the runtime registry or the bundled-only path. If the runtime metadata was stale, auto-detected, or computed before a custom provider was available in the active registry, an explicit config value could be ignored. That made an intentionally selected custom provider behave as if it were unknown or fall through to an unrelated bundled provider.

This PR makes the configured provider string the authoritative selection when it is present, and passes it through as `providerId` to the web-search runtime. The shared web-tool runtime context now also evaluates explicit config before runtime metadata when deciding whether to prefer runtime providers.

## Why This Is Needed

`tools.web.search.provider` is a user-facing explicit routing setting. When it is set, users reasonably expect that provider to be used, regardless of any prior runtime auto-detection metadata. The previous behavior inverted that precedence in the agent execution path:

- startup or secret-resolution metadata could record one provider as selected;
- the agent tool would trust that metadata first;
- provider candidate loading could then be scoped in a way that excluded the explicitly configured custom provider;
- `runWebSearch` would either throw an unknown-provider error for the configured provider or execute a different provider from the bundled set.

That is both surprising and risky. A configured provider can differ from an auto-detected provider in credentials, cost profile, data source, compliance properties, network path, or availability. Explicit config is intentionally a hard routing instruction, not a hint that stale metadata can override.

## What Changed

- `createWebSearchTool` now reads the configured `tools.web.search.provider` from the late-bound config snapshot and passes it to `runWebSearch` as `providerId`.
- `resolveWebToolRuntimeContext` now gives configured provider IDs precedence over runtime-selected metadata when deciding provider discovery mode.
- `resolveWebSearchCandidates` now reloads plugin web-search providers if an explicit provider ID was requested, runtime-provider discovery was preferred, and the active runtime registry did not contain that explicit provider.
- `docs/tools/web.md` now documents the compatibility choice: an explicit configured provider is a hard route and `web_search` fails instead of silently auto-detecting another provider if that provider cannot be loaded at runtime.

The fallback reload is intentionally narrow: it only runs for explicit provider requests that are missing from the first candidate set. Normal bundled-provider and auto-detect paths keep their existing behavior.

## Impact

Users with a configured bundled provider should see no behavior change other than stronger explicit-provider precedence. Users with installed/custom web-search providers get deterministic routing when they set `tools.web.search.provider`.

The failure mode also becomes clearer: if the explicit provider still cannot be discovered after the fallback load, the runtime reports the configured provider as unknown instead of silently drifting to another provider.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent `web_search` should honor an explicit `tools.web.search.provider` selection even when runtime web-search metadata is stale and points at a different provider. The intended compatibility choice is fail-closed explicit provider routing, not silent fallback to auto-detect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repo runtime on Linux in this isolated PR worktree, using production TypeScript modules via `pnpm exec tsx`; no gateway restart, no host config writes, and no real credentials.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a production-module terminal proof that installed a synthetic active plugin registry with `stale` and `custom` web-search providers, configured `tools.web.search.provider: "custom"`, supplied stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`, and executed the agent `web_search` tool with query `routing proof`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the production-module proof:

```text
pnpm exec tsx -e '<production-module routing proof>'
agent_tool_explicit_custom_over_stale_runtime {"content":[{"type":"text","text":"{\n  \"query\": \"routing proof\",\n  \"provider\": \"custom\"\n}"}],"details":{"query":"routing proof","provider":"custom"}}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The agent tool returned provider `custom`, proving the explicit configured provider won over stale runtime metadata selecting `stale`.
- **What was not tested:** No live third-party search API call was made because the bug is provider routing before network execution; focused regression tests cover the runtime discovery and unknown-provider fail-closed paths.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/web.md src/agents/tools/web-search.ts src/agents/tools/web-tool-runtime-context.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/web-search.test.ts src/agents/tools/web-tools.enabled-defaults.test.ts src/web-search/runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json` selected `core`, `coreTests`, and `docs`.
- `OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK=1 OPENCLAW_LOCAL_CHECK_MODE=throttled pnpm check:changed` was attempted because Testbox is not authenticated here; it completed conflict-marker, changelog attribution, dependency/patch guards, core typecheck, core test typecheck, core lint, runtime sidecar loader, and runtime import-cycle checks, then left an idle node process with no active child work and was stopped to avoid leaving it behind.
- `pnpm check:docs`

Testbox was attempted first but is unavailable in this environment:

```text
blacksmith testbox warmup ci-check-testbox.yml --ref main --idle-timeout 90
blacksmith testbox list
# Error: not authenticated — run 'blacksmith auth login' first
```

Known unrelated broad-check note: `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` currently reports `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` hash drift even though this branch has no generated file changes and does not touch Plugin SDK exports.

target.title: fix: typing TTL, context display, rate limit messages, slack catch-up reconnect
target.body:
## Summary

Several bug fixes and improvements discovered during production use:

### Bug fixes
- **Typing TTL too short** — default 60s TTL caused typing indicators to expire during long LLM responses; increased to 20min (`typing.ts`)
- **Context token display inaccurate** — removed unreliable fallback estimation, added freshness marker, reset display on compact (`status.ts`)
- **Rate limit error messages missing context** — now includes provider name, model, and parsed retry-after seconds (`errors.ts`)
- **Compaction notifications hidden** — always show compaction notifications regardless of verbose mode (`agent-runner.ts`, `followup-runner.ts`)
- **Post-compact context incorrect** — use `resolveContextTokensForModel` to get accurate context tokens after compaction (`commands-compact.ts`)
- **Status display stale data** — prevent stale transcript from overwriting fresh session data, cap values at context window size (`status.ts`)

### New feature
- **Slack catch-up on reconnect** — after socket disconnect/reconnect, fetch missed messages that mention the bot and replay them. Prevents "missed message" gaps during brief reboots (typically 5-15s window). New file: `slack/monitor/catch-up.ts` (174 lines)

## Test plan
- [ ] Verify typing indicator persists during long responses (>60s)
- [ ] Confirm context token display updates correctly after compaction
- [ ] Test rate limit error includes provider/model info
- [ ] Simulate Slack socket disconnect and verify catch-up replays missed @mentions

target.title: fix(exec): resume agent turn for native chat exec approvals
target.body: ## Summary Native chat exec approvals (Telegram `/approve allow-once`, Discord buttons, Slack actions, etc.) were resolved by the gateway but never resumed the agent's turn. The exec tool returned the `approval-pending` tool result, the run terminated, the background `sendExecApprovalFollowup` either fell through to a direct delivery to the operator or failed silently, and the model never saw an `Exec running / Exec finished / Exec denied` event. Operators had to send a follow-up chat message to recover the turn, and a new approval was minted because the original run had already ended — producing the "approval → 催促 → 新审批" loop. This extends the inline approval-pending path that PR #85239 added for webchat to every bundled chat channel that ships an `approval-handler.runtime` adapter. When the originating turn can be approved in the same chat, the gateway resolves the approval in place and the agent waits inline for the command output instead of terminating on `approval-pending`. closes:https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93918 ## Behavior Before: 1. Agent calls exec → `processGatewayAllowlist` registers approval → tool returns `pendingResult` (fire-and-forget) → agent run ends. 2. Operator approves in chat → background block runs the command → `sendExecApprovalFollowup` tries to resume the agent via `callGatewayTool("agent", ...)`. 3. In practice the resume either fails or falls through to a direct delivery to the operator; the agent never sees the result and stays in the "pending" view. 4. Operator sends any chat message → agent re-runs the original task → fresh allowlist-miss → new approval ID → loop. After: 1. Agent calls exec → `shouldAwaitGatewayApprovalInline` returns `true` for native chat channels (when `unavailableReason === null` and no explicit `approvalFollowupMode`) → tool awaits the decision in place. 2. Operator approves in chat → `resolveApprovalDecisionOrUndefined` resolves inline → command runs in the same tool call → real `Exec finished (gateway id=…, code 0)` text is the tool result the model reads. 3. If the operator denies, the inline `deniedResult` returns `Exec denied (gateway id=…, user-denied): …` as the tool result; no background followup is needed. 4. Channels without a native chat approval client (e.g. `feishu`, `msteams`, `line`, headless `cron`/`heartbeat`) still take the fire-and-forget path; explicit `approvalFollowupMode: "agent"` / `"direct"` opt-out is preserved. ## Change - New `NATIVE_APPROVAL_CHANNELS` list and `isNativeApprovalChannel` helper in `src/utils/message-channel-constants.ts` (`webchat`, `discord`, `googlechat`, `imessage`, `matrix`, `qqbot`, `signal`, `slack`, `telegram`, `whatsapp`). The list is kept in lockstep with the bundled extensions that publish an `approval-handler.runtime` adapter and a `resolveApproveCommandBehavior` capability. - `shouldAwaitGatewayApprovalInline` in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts:294-308` now consults `isNativeApprovalChannel` instead of comparing against `INTERNAL_MESSAGE_CHANNEL` directly. The `approvalFollowupMode` opt-out and the `unavailableReason === null` gate are preserved, so the new path is unreachable for cron / headless / disabled-native-approval cases. - Re-exports the new helpers from `src/utils/message-channel.ts`. ## Verification Local: - `pnpm tsgo:core` — clean. - `pnpm tsgo:extensions` — clean. - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/utils/message-channel.test.ts` — 7/7 (new `NATIVE_APPROVAL_CHANNELS` membership test included). - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts` — 46/46 (18 new parameterized cases covering 9 native channels × inline-resolution + inline-denial, plus regression cases for `feishu` and `approvalFollowupMode: "agent"` keeping the fire-and-forget path). - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-followup.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-approval-request.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts` — 66/66. - `pnpm lint:core` (oxlint on touched files) — no findings. - `pnpm format:check` on touched files — clean (the 55 pre-existing format warnings are unrelated to this branch and reproduce on `main`). ## Real behavior proof Behavior addressed: Native chat exec approvals in the originating chat thread now resume the original agent turn inline instead of ending on `approval-pending` and forcing the operator to send another message that mints a fresh approval. Real environment tested: macOS 26.4.1 with a real Telegram client talking to real Telegram bot `@NianJiuopenclawbot`, local build of `openclaw` from PR head `2f7b360d54c762750b556253b940f9719b6ba0a3`, with `tools.exec.host=gateway` and `tools.exec.ask=always`. Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Started the PR build locally with gateway exec approvals forced on. 2. From a real Telegram chat, sent `请用 exec 工具执行 date +%s%N 并把纳秒时间戳告诉我`. 3. Waited for the approval card, clicked `Allow once`, and watched the same Telegram thread for the post-approval reply. Evidence after fix: - Screenshot from the real Telegram thread after approval: - Redacted live runtime output from the same run: - `22:21:30 UTC+8 — telegram outbound sendMessage messageId=104` - `22:23:38 — exec.approval.waitDecision blocked 132s in blocked_tool_call` - `22:23:52 — exec.approval.waitDecision 145500ms resolved after Allow once` - `Exec finished (gateway id=…, code 0)` followed by the 19-digit nanosecond timestamp in the same turn. Observed result after fix: The original agent turn stayed open while waiting for approval, the command ran immediately after `Allow once`, and the bot replied in the same Telegram thread with `Exec finished (gateway id=…, code 0)` plus the timestamp. No extra wake-up message was needed and no duplicate `Run:` approval prompt appeared. What was not tested: I did not rerun the same live proof on Slack, Discord, Google Chat, or a deny-path click for this PR body update; those channel/deny branches are covered here by the targeted tests listed in the Verification section. ## Related - Refs https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93918 - Builds on PR #85239 (`fix(exec): return approved WebChat gateway exec output inline`) — same root cause, webchat path only. - Same root cause as #39648 (Control UI + multi-agent TUI path on 2026.3.2). This PR covers the native chat path on 2026.6.6+; the two surfaces share the same `shouldAwaitGatewayApprovalInline` decision now.

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: open, download, and copy
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably
- Binds paired Control UI read-token media fetches to the requested session key before serving managed image bytes
- Renders generated image frames with the same rounded clipping as other chat media
- Pre-scales known generated-image dimensions so the frame matches the visible thumbnail/image size
- Reserves pending preview space while managed image blobs load, preventing chat scroll/window jumps
- Serves timeline images from configurable thumbnail routes while keeping open/download/copy on the full image
- Adds `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` for local/managed Control UI thumbnails
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract

## Why

Generated images were being displayed without the full media affordances we expect in chat. The implementation also needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry so the new actions attach to the visible image, rounded corners clip the actual image, and the chat viewport does not jump while a managed preview is still loading.

The follow-up fixes make the preview/full-image split explicit: right-click View Image uses the thumbnail preview, while click/Open/Download/Copy use the original full-size image.

## Testing

target.title: fix: harden subagent completion announce cleanup races
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a set of subagent completion lifecycle races that can leave completed child runs stuck behind cleanup/announce sequencing or overwrite a terminal error with a later success signal.

The patch makes three related changes:

- Do not block completion announce delivery on browser-session cleanup. The cleanup is now started after the announce/cleanup flow is leased and runs best-effort in the background, with warnings captured instead of blocking the lifecycle path.
- Skip waiting for an active embedded child session when a frozen final reply is already available. If we already captured the reply text, the requester can be notified immediately instead of waiting on a stale/active child run check.
- Preserve a terminal error outcome if a later completion signal reports success for the same run, unless the run was actually restarted after the error. This prevents cleanup/finalization races from turning a failed run into a successful task after the fact.

## Why this matters

The lifecycle path has a few asynchronous pieces that can finish in different orders:

1. child session/browser cleanup
2. completion announce delivery back to the requester
3. task finalization bookkeeping
4. embedded child-run settle checks
5. terminal lifecycle hooks

Before this patch, a slow or stuck browser cleanup could delay the announce/cleanup flow. Separately, if a child run had already produced frozen reply text but still appeared active, the announce flow could wait unnecessarily. And in a failure path, a later `complete` event could overwrite the already-recorded terminal error.

That combination makes subagent completion feel flaky from the user's side: the work is done, but the parent session may not receive the completion promptly, or the persisted final task state can become misleading.

## Implementation notes

### Lifecycle cleanup lease

`completeSubagentRun` now preclaims cleanup when `triggerCleanup` is true and cleanup has not already completed/been handled. That lease is passed into `startSubagentAnnounceCleanupFlow` as `skipBeginCleanup`, avoiding a second `beginSubagentCleanup` race while still protecting duplicate cleanup attempts.

If cleanup is not actually triggered, or announce is suppressed for steer-restart, the preclaim is released so the record does not stay permanently handled.

### Browser cleanup no longer blocks announce cleanup

Previously browser cleanup was awaited before starting the subagent announce cleanup flow. This patch starts the announce cleanup flow first, retires run-mode bundle MCP runtime as before, and then launches browser cleanup as a best-effort async task:

- uses injected `cleanupBrowserSessionsForLifecycleEnd` when provided
- lazy-loads the browser cleanup helper otherwise
- logs warning context on failure
- does not reject or delay completion delivery

### Frozen reply short-circuit

`runSubagentAnnounceFlow` now checks whether `roundOneReply` already contains non-empty frozen reply text. If it does, the flow does not wait for `waitForEmbeddedPiRunEnd` even if `isEmbeddedPiRunActive` reports true. The frozen reply is already the durable completion payload; waiting only increases the chance of a stale active-state race.

### Terminal error preservation

When a run already has a terminal error outcome/reason, a later successful completion signal no longer overwrites it unless the entry's `startedAt` proves the run restarted after the error's `endedAt`. The ended hook and persisted outcome use the preserved effective reason/outcome.

## Tests

Added/updated coverage for:

- announcing without waiting for browser cleanup
- skipping announce delivery when completion messages are disabled
- delete-mode cleanup when completion messages are disabled
- preserving terminal errors against later success completion
- allowing success to replace error only after a restart
- using frozen reply text without waiting on an active embedded child session

Verification run locally:

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/subagent-registry-lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts
```

Result: 2 files passed, 29 tests passed.

target.title: fix #93501: [Bug]: Matrix error decrypting event
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Matrix SDK missing-key decryption failures can surface through `sync.unexpected_error`; the monitor lifecycle treated every unexpected sync error as fatal, so the Matrix channel stopped even though this encrypted event failure is recoverable at the per-event boundary.
- Solution: Classify only the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event error as nonfatal in the Matrix monitor lifecycle fatal-stop path.
- What changed: `sync.unexpected_error` now ignores Matrix missing-key `m.room.encrypted` decryption errors, while ordinary unexpected sync errors still record an error and stop the channel task.
- What did not change: No retry policy, crypto state, Matrix SDK setup, account config, gateway routing, channel delivery contract, outbound send behavior, provider routing, or session-state persistence changed.

## Why it matters / User impact

When a Matrix room contains an encrypted event whose keys have not reached the bot device yet, the user currently loses Matrix gateway communication until they manually recover the bot session and restart the gateway. That is a channel-level outage caused by a per-event decrypt failure. Keeping the monitor alive for this specific missing-key encrypted event lets later Matrix sync work continue while preserving fatal handling for real SDK or account failures.

## Scope boundary

- In scope: the Matrix monitor lifecycle decision that handles `sync.unexpected_error` and decides whether `waitForFatalStop()` should reject.
- In scope: one regression test for the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted event message.
- Out of scope: changing Matrix decrypt retry counts, key backup/session recovery, crypto storage, account verification, gateway routing, provider/model routing, plugin hooks, channel delivery semantics, and any public configuration/default/schema/protocol surface.
- Failure-mode boundary: only an error message that contains the encrypted event context (`error decrypting event` + `m.room.encrypted`) and the missing-key decrypt reason (`DecryptionError`/`MEGOLM_UNKNOWN_INBOUND_SESSION_ID` plus the missing key reason) is treated as nonfatal. Ordinary `sync.unexpected_error` remains fatal.

## Source-of-truth boundary

The source of truth for the outage is `createMatrixMonitorSyncLifecycle`: it subscribes to the Matrix SDK `sync.unexpected_error` event and currently calls `statusController.noteUnexpectedError(error)` plus `settleFatal(error)` for every such error. This patch changes only that lifecycle fatal-stop boundary. It does not move responsibility into downstream status rendering or upstream Matrix SDK/decrypt retry behavior.

## Competition / linked PR analysis

- Related open PR: #94044 (`fix(matrix): prevent gateway stop on Megolm missing-key decryption failures`).
- Same user issue, different patch shape: #94044 classifies broad message buckets (`decrypt`, `megolm`, `network`, `timeout`, `offline`, `m_unknown_token`, `m_limit_exceeded`) as recoverable and also changes decrypt retry attempts for `MEGOLM_UNKNOWN_INBOUND_SESSION_ID`.
- Why this PR is intentionally narrower: the reported outage happens when an issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event decrypt error reaches the monitor lifecycle fatal-stop path. This patch fixes that exact source-of-truth boundary without changing retry policy, network/token error handling, crypto state, or account recovery behavior.
- Why this is safer to review: the runtime proof below includes a negative-control ordinary `sync.unexpected_error` probe, which still rejects `waitForFatalStop()` and records `healthState: "error"`. That guards against turning unrelated Matrix sync, token, timeout, or SDK failures into silent recoverable events.
- Non-duplication claim: this is not a broader canonical retry-policy patch; it is the minimal lifecycle root-cause fix for #93501 with a local real-behavior proof of both the fixed missing-key path and the unchanged fatal path.

## Origin / follow-up

N/A — independent root-cause PR for #93501. #94044 is an external competing open PR for the same issue, not a merged origin/follow-up anchor.

Closes #93501

## Patch quality notes

- Diff size is intentionally small: one production file and one matching regression test file.
- The matcher is conjunctive instead of a broad `decrypt`/`megolm` substring allowlist, so unrelated SDK, token, timeout, network, and account errors are not reclassified.
- The patch does not add fallback state, retry loops, compatibility shims, public options, schema changes, or new gateway/channel behavior.
- The negative-control runtime probe and existing fatal-path test coverage explain the public-contract warning around `sync.unexpected_error`: the event contract still treats ordinary unexpected sync errors as fatal; only the specific missing-key encrypted-event failure is excluded.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Matrix missing-key encrypted-event decryption errors should not stop the Matrix channel monitor; unrelated unexpected sync errors must remain fatal.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux worktree at PR head `8411e6fc8bb88b816faa7b28bf2a5c77dbb905bf`, fetched `origin/main` `bdc46fa28de3364ed95d89e6916002ca159fcd6e`, Node `v22.22.0`, pnpm dependencies installed from the lockfile. The proof drives the Matrix monitor lifecycle at the SDK event boundary by emitting `sync.unexpected_error` on a Matrix-client-compatible event emitter.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" install --frozen-lockfile
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" && \
  node --import tsx "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-93501-evidence/matrix-missing-key-sync-proof.mjs" | \
  tee "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-93501-evidence/matrix-missing-key-sync-proof.output.json"
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" && \
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/sync-lifecycle.test.ts \
    extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.test.ts
```

```bash
git -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-93501" diff --check
```

- **Evidence after fix:** The Matrix missing-key scenario kept the fatal wait pending and produced no error status. The ordinary unexpected error probe still rejected and recorded an error.

```json
{
  "results": [
    {
      "scenario": "missing-key-decryption",
      "waitBeforeDispose": "pending",
      "errorStatusCount": 0,
      "lastHealthState": "starting"
    },
    {
      "scenario": "ordinary-unexpected-error",
      "waitResult": "rejected:sync exploded",
      "errorStatusCount": 1,
      "lastError": "sync exploded"
    }
  ]
}
```

The focused monitor suites passed:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  52 passed (52)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 87.22s
```

`diff --check` passed with no output.

- **Observed result after fix:** The Matrix monitor lifecycle no longer treats the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event decryption error as a fatal channel stop, and the adjacent fatal error path remains unchanged for ordinary unexpected sync errors.
- **What was not tested:** No live production Matrix account, Element session, external homeserver, or full Docker-backed Matrix QA lane was used. The proof isolates the SDK `sync.unexpected_error` event boundary that caused the monitor task to stop.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix for the monitor fatal-stop boundary.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/sync-lifecycle.test.ts`
- **Regression scenario:** A `sync.unexpected_error` carrying `Error decrypting event ... type=m.room.encrypted ... DecryptionError ... has not sent us the keys` must not set Matrix monitor error health and must keep `waitForFatalStop()` alive until normal disposal.
- **Test guardrail rationale:** The same lifecycle suite already covers the fatal path for ordinary `sync.unexpected_error`; keeping that coverage plus the runtime negative-control probe prevents the fix from becoming a broad error-swallowing fallback.
- `extensions/matrix/src/matrix/monitor/index.test.ts` was run as the closest monitor integration coverage around status/lifecycle wiring.
- The regression test was added before the production fix and failed on the current implementation with `AssertionError: expected true to be false`, then passed after the lifecycle filter was added.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** `channel: matrix`, existing related PR #94044, message-delivery behavior, session-state health reporting, and external Matrix live-proof boundary.
- **Risk explanation:** The changed code is in the Matrix monitor lifecycle fatal-stop filter. An over-broad filter could hide real sync failures or make the channel look healthy when the SDK/account is actually broken.
- **Why acceptable:** The matcher is intentionally narrow to the issue-shaped missing-key encrypted-event error, and both the runtime proof and existing tests show unrelated unexpected sync errors still stop the monitor and record an error.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** `createMatrixMonitorSyncLifecycle` treated every `sync.unexpected_error` from the Matrix SDK as fatal. Missing-key encrypted-event decrypt errors are per-event recoverable failures already handled by Matrix decryption/failed-decryption paths, but the lifecycle layer converted them into a channel-level fatal stop.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The fix is applied at the lifecycle fatal-stop boundary where the channel was being stopped, rather than broadening decrypt retry behavior or changing Matrix account/crypto state.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the targeted lifecycle bug; live production Matrix proof is not included and is disclosed above.

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(status): show session costs
target.body:
## Summary
- Use transcript `usage.cost.total` for `/status` cost when available, so the status card can show cumulative transcript-backed session cost instead of only a pricing-table estimate.
- Add a conditional `Cost` column to `openclaw status` recent session rows when at least one shown session has persisted cost.
- Update status/cost docs for the new behavior.

## Linked context
Fixes #64334.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
Behavior addressed: `/status` and `openclaw status` now surface existing session cost data.
Real environment tested: Local source checkout on macOS using an isolated temporary OpenClaw state directory with one session store and transcript; CLI launched with temporary Node v24.16.0 because the system Node v22.15.0 is below OpenClaw's runtime guard.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_HOME="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/status-proof-94308/home" OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/status-proof-94308/state" PATH="/var/folders/6l/gn1ygj3d28s_pdrxjqkzc1440000gn/T/opencode/corepack-bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm dlx node@24 openclaw.mjs status`
Evidence after fix: Real CLI output included the Sessions table below from the isolated OpenClaw state:

```text
Sessions
┌───────────────────────────────────┬────────┬─────────┬─────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────┬─────────┐
│ Key                               │ Kind   │ Age     │ Model           │ Runtime          │ Tokens         │ Cost    │
├───────────────────────────────────┼────────┼─────────┼─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────┼─────────┤
│ agent:main:main                   │ direct │ unknown │ claude-opus-4-6 │ OpenClaw Default │ 45/32k (0%)    │ $0.0063 │
└───────────────────────────────────┴────────┴─────────┴─────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────┴─────────┘
```

Observed result after fix: The actual `openclaw status` path rendered the new `Cost` column and displayed `$0.0063`, matching the cumulative transcript usage costs `0.0021 + 0.0042` stored in the isolated session transcript.
What was not tested: Full test suite; local default Node is below the repo-requested `>=22.19.0`, so `corepack pnpm ...` validation commands emitted engine warnings unless the proof command used temporary Node 24.

## Additional verification
- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `corepack pnpm check:docs`
- `corepack pnpm test src/commands/status.test.ts src/commands/status.command-sections.test.ts src/commands/status.command-report-data.test.ts src/auto-reply/status.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-shared.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm format:check src/status/status-message.ts src/auto-reply/status.test.ts src/commands/status.command-sections.ts src/commands/status.command-report-data.ts src/commands/status.summary.ts src/commands/status.types.ts src/commands/status.command-sections.test.ts src/commands/status.command-report-data.test.ts src/commands/status.test-support.ts src/commands/status.test.ts`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`

## Known proof notes
- The branch was rebased onto current `origin/main`; the previous plugin-sdk CI failure in `src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-shared.test.ts` passes on the rebased branch.
- Global `corepack pnpm format:check` reports unrelated pre-existing formatting issues outside the touched files; the changed source files pass the scoped formatter check.

target.title: fix: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typin...
target.body:
## Summary

Found one concrete regression: making typing start fire-and-forget allows cleanup/idle to run before a persistent typing indicator has finished starting, so the stop path can no-op and leave the indicator behind.

## What ClawSweeper Is Fixing

- **Medium: Pending typing start can outlive cleanup and leave persistent indicators stuck** (regression)
  - File: `src/channels/typing.ts:79`
  - Evidence: `onReplyStart` now starts `fireStart()` in the background and returns after one microtask at `src/channels/typing.ts:79-87`, while `fireStop` immediately marks the callbacks closed and calls `stop` at `src/channels/typing.ts:90-98`. For Feishu, `start` assigns `typingState` only after `await addTypingIndicator(...)` at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:176`; `stop` returns without doing anything if `typingState` is still unset at `extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts:183-185`. A fast cleanup while `addTypingIndicator` is still in flight therefore calls stop too early, then the delayed start completes and records a persistent reaction with no later removal.
  - Impact: Feishu typing reactions are explicitly persistent until removed. A quick reply, `NO_REPLY`, empty-message cleanup, cancellation, or send-policy path can leave a visible typing reaction/indicator on the parent message after the run is already finished.
  - Suggested fix: keep typing start off the reply critical path, but serialize cleanup against the pending start. For example, track the current start promise and, if stop/cleanup happens while it is pending, run `stop` again after the promise settles when a start actually completed. Alternatively make persistent-indicator channels expose an abortable/pending-aware start helper.
  - Confidence: high

## Expected Repair Surface

- `src/channels/typing.ts`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`
- `src/channels/typing.test.ts`

## Source And Review Context

- ClawSweeper report: https://github.com/openclaw/clawsweeper/blob/main/records/openclaw-openclaw/commits/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d.md
- Commit under review: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d
- Latest main at intake: 8093ae60292f4d9009b3d48f234b39b5cbc538e5
- Original commit author: Ayaan Zaidi
- GitHub author: @obviyus
- Highest severity: medium
- Review confidence: high

- Diff: `40b0b1bfe051072f47d0160eb078afa3b17cef48..45b86450795d8bd3d1e548c8815cd97d6583199d`
- Changed files: `src/auto-reply/reply/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.ts`, `src/channels/typing.test.ts`
- Code read: changed files in full; `src/channels/typing-lifecycle.ts`; `src/channels/typing-start-guard.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-dispatcher.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts`; `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts`; relevant channel typing call sites, including Feishu, Matrix, Telegram, Discord, Signal, Mattermost, MSTeams, Zalo, and heartbeat typing.
- Dependencies/web: no dependency files changed; no web lookup needed.

## Expected validation

- `pnpm check:changed`

ClawSweeper already ran:
- `pnpm install` was needed because `vitest/package.json` was missing.
- `pnpm test src/channels/typing.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/typing-persistence.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` passed.
- Focused `pnpm exec tsx -e ...` race probe reproduced the stale persistent-state ordering.

Known review limits:
- I did not run a live Feishu API test; the finding is based on the current callback ordering and Feishu’s local persistent-reaction state contract.

## ClawSweeper Guardrails

- Re-check the finding against latest `main` before changing code.
- Keep the patch to the narrowest behavior change and matching regression coverage.
- Do not merge automatically; this PR stays for maintainer review.
